text:
Jack was a teenager with a love for the outdoors. Every weekend, he could be found hiking through forests, exploring deserted beaches, or catching frogs. There was nothing he found more thrilling than that strange connection he felt with nature.

One particular summer, Jack enrolled in an outdoor exploration course. The instructor, a rugged veteran hiker named Ms. Jenkins, was adamant about preserving the natural world. She talked endlessly about the intricate patterns on a spider's web and the deep meaning behind hummingbirds visiting a blooming wild rose.

Despite Ms. Jenkins' warnings about hawk nesting boxes, Jack kept putting them near bird-feeders. One afternoon, after a grueling hike through an ancient pine forest, he stumbled onto something unusual—three enormous owls circling a hawk trapped in a net.

Staring at them, Jack couldn't decide if he was relieved or astonished. Then, on the owl's right wing, he noticed the most beautiful scarlet feather he'd ever seen. Jack carefully reached in
